advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the stale 'API STUB — bodies filled by the implementer' line from hmac_legacy.py's module docstring since the implementation is complete.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py:14: `API STUB — bodies filled by the implementer.`
Bodies are present: `expected = hmac.new(key, pae_bytes, hashlib.sha256).digest()` at hmac_legacy.py:59 and full register_legacy_schemes() at hmac_legacy.py:69-77.
advisory.finding: The module docstring still says 'API STUB — bodies filled by the implementer', but the HmacScheme.verify and register_legacy_schemes bodies are fully implemented in this same file. The stale STUB marker will mislead future readers into thinking the module is unfinished.
advisory.suggested_fix: Delete the 'API STUB — bodies filled by the implementer.' line from the module docstring.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider handling a non-bytes trust_root in HmacScheme.verify (fail closed) rather than letting hmac.new raise TypeError, since the protocol types trust_root as Any.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py:58: `key = cast(bytes, trust_root)  # trust_root is the HMAC key (bytes).`
registry.py:61 types the parameter as `trust_root: Any,` in the Scheme protocol — no static bytes guarantee.
The ticket contract documents trust_root as the HMAC key bytes, so this is a documented boundary rather than a contract violation.
advisory.finding: HmacScheme.verify casts trust_root to bytes (`key = cast(bytes, trust_root)`) after only a falsy-check, then calls hmac.new(key, ...). If a caller supplies a non-bytes truthy key (e.g. a str), hmac.new raises TypeError instead of returning a fail-closed Verdict. The registry Scheme protocol types trust_root as Any, so the type is not statically guaranteed at the call boundary.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test that a full DSSE envelope for a pinned kind certifies through `registry.verify("plan-review", envelope, key)` (and mismatches on a wrong key), exercising the registration+policy wiring end-to-end.
evidence: Test calls the scheme directly: `verdict = hmac_legacy.HmacScheme().verify(pae_bytes, [sig], "rebar-attest-hmac", key)` (test_attest_hmac_legacy.py:36)
Registration test only asserts presence/pinning: `policy = registry.resolve(kind)` / `assert policy.scheme == "HMAC-SHA256"` (test_attest_hmac_legacy.py:25-27)

ABSENCE: no call to `registry.verify("plan-review", envelope, key)` in either new test file; registry.verify dispatch is only tested with a FAKE scheme in tests/unit/test_attest_registry*.py, so the real HmacScheme's integration with the pinned policy is not exercised end-to-end.

registry.verify passes `envelope.pae(), list(envelope.signatures), policy.namespace, trust_root` to the scheme (registry.py:110-115) — a mismatch in how a real envelope's signatures/pae reach HmacScheme would go undetected.
advisory.finding: No end-to-end test that a pinned legacy kind certifies through registry.verify(kind, envelope, trust_root) — the integration behavior this diff's registration+policy pinning enables. Tests assert the scheme is registered and the policy is pinned (structure) and separately call HmacScheme().verify(...) directly (unit), but never dispatch a real DSSE envelope for a pinned kind through the registry to confirm the wiring certifies.
scenarios: A regression in registry.verify's dispatch (wrong arg order, or POLICY resolution for the pinned kind) that breaks certification of a real envelope for 'plan-review' / 'completion-verifier'
A namespace/kind wiring drift between the pinned Policy and the scheme
advisory.suggested_fix: Add a test building a dsse.Envelope with signatures[0].sig = hmac_sha256(key, envelope.pae()) and assert `registry.verify("plan-review", envelope, key).verified is True` and `.verdict == "certified"`, plus a wrong-key case asserting mismatch.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Bind the policy-pinned `namespace` into HmacScheme.verify's HMAC computation (e.g. domain-separated prefix over namespace + pae_bytes) so per-kind domain separation is enforced by the MAC as the registry contract promises, or explicitly document that PAE payload_type is the sole separator and enforce a distinct payload_type per kind.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py:59: `expected = hmac.new(key, pae_bytes, hashlib.sha256).digest()` — computes the MAC over pae_bytes only; the `namespace` param (line 42) is never referenced in the body.
registry.py:16-19 contract: 'The scheme is invoked over the DSSE-PAE bytes of the envelope and the policy-pinned ``namespace``' — the namespace is declared verified material.

src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py:79: both LEGACY_KINDS are pinned to `namespace=HMAC_NAMESPACE` ('rebar-attest-hmac'), and registry.verify (registry.py:110-114) supplies that same namespace + a single caller-supplied trust_root key to verify() — so nothing in the MAC distinguishes one kind from another.
src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py:26 docstring calls HMAC_NAMESPACE 'The domain-separation namespace', asserting a separation property the verify() body does not implement.
advisory.finding: HmacScheme.verify accepts the policy-pinned `namespace` argument but never binds it into the HMAC computation, contradicting the registry Scheme contract which states the scheme is invoked over the PAE bytes AND the policy-pinned namespace. Domain separation between the two pinned kinds (plan-review, completion-verifier) — which share the same namespace and the same trust_root key — is not enforced by the MAC; it rests solely on the DSSE payload_type inside the PAE bytes, which this scheme neither checks nor ties to the namespace/kind.
scenarios: A signature/PAE pair certified under the plan-review kind is replayed as a completion-verifier attestation (or vice versa) when the two kinds produce the same DSSE payload_type/body, since the shared namespace and key make the MACs identical and the namespace is never mixed in.
A future third kind added to LEGACY_KINDS with a colliding payload_type inherits the same cross-kind acceptance.
